claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 12 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 12 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)

Child is not in local authority care

Child has not started an apprenticeship in England

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

First, let me review the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children under the age of 16, or children aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! Yes, I'd like to know if I'm eligible to claim Child Benefit. 

I have two children - Alex who is 12, and Blake who is 6.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have two children - Alex (aged 12) and Blake (aged 6).

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for each of them, I need to ask a few questions.

**Do Alex and Blake currently live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, yes. Blake lives with me, but Alex doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So Blake (aged 6) lives with you in the UK, and Alex (aged 12) does not live with you.

**Could you tell me where Alex lives? Does Alex live with someone else, such as the other parent, a relative, or a friend?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with their other parent.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. So Alex lives with their other parent.

**Are you currently making any financial contributions towards Alex's upkeep? This could include things like paying for clothes, food, pocket money, presents, or contributing to their accommodation costs. If so, how much do you contribute and how often?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do help out with Alex. I contribute towards things like clothes and bits and bobs for school. I give about £20 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. You're contributing about £20 per week towards Alex's upkeep for things like clothes and school items.

Just to ensure I have all the information I need:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex's other parent - the one Alex lives with - they already claim Child Benefit for Alex. And for Blake... well, Blake lives with me, but their other parent claims the Child Benefit for Blake too.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. So to summarize:
- Alex lives with their other parent, who claims Child Benefit for Alex
- Blake lives with you, but their other parent claims Child Benefit for Blake

Just to make sure I understand the situation fully:

**Regarding Blake - even though Blake lives with you, the other parent is claiming Child Benefit. Are you and Blake's other parent living together, or are you living separately?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: We're living separately. Blake lives here with me, but their other parent claims the Child Benefit.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clarification. I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary of Your Child Benefit Eligibility

**For Alex (aged 12):** You are **not eligible** to claim Child Benefit. Alex lives with their other parent, who is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any time. While you are contributing £20 per week towards Alex's upkeep, this is less than the Child Benefit rate, and regardless, you cannot claim while someone else is already claiming for the same child.

**For Blake (aged 6):** You **are eligible** to claim Child Benefit. Blake lives with you, which means you are responsible for Blake. Although Blake's other parent is currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake, since Blake lives with you, you have the stronger claim under the rules. You would need to contact the Child Benefit Office to resolve this situation. They will help sort out who should claim, but as the person Blake lives with, you would be the eligible claimant. The other parent would need to stop their claim for you to receive Child Benefit for Blake.

**Next Steps:** If you want to claim Child Benefit for Blake, you should contact the Child Benefit Office to explain that Blake lives with you and that you wish to claim. They will help resolve the situation with the other parent's existing claim.

reasonings: Alex is 12 years old and lives with their other parent, who is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child at any time. While you are contributing £20 per week towards Alex's upkeep (for clothes and school items), this amount is less than the current Child Benefit rate (which is £25.60 per week for the eldest/only child, or £16.95 per week for additional children). Even if your contribution met the threshold, you cannot claim Child Benefit while someone else is already claiming it for the same child. Therefore, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 6 years old and lives with you in the UK. Under Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. Since Blake lives with you, you are responsible for Blake and meet the basic eligibility criteria. Although Blake's other parent is currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any time. However, since you are the person Blake lives with, you have the stronger claim. You would need to contact the Child Benefit Office to resolve this situation - they would help determine who should claim, but as the person Blake lives with, you would be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake. The other parent would need to stop claiming for this to happen.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (12) who lives with the other parent, and Blake (6) who lives with the claimant. Alex's other parent already claims Child Benefit for Alex, and the claimant's contribution of £20/week is insufficient to maintain eligibility under the contribution rules. The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. However, for Blake, who lives with the claimant, the claimant meets the eligibility criteria as they are responsible for Blake. Although Blake's other parent currently claims Child Benefit for Blake, only one person can claim per child, and the claimant (as the person Blake lives with) has the stronger claim and would be eligible once the current claim is resolved with the Child Benefit Office.

The other parent would need to stop claiming for this to happen.
